items.question: What does the Android manifest declare about an app?
items.correctAnswer: Components, permissions, and essential metadata
incorrectAnswers: Only the app icon pixel data
items.explanation: AndroidManifest.xml registers components and required capabilities for the system.

items.question: What file format packages Android apps for distribution?
items.correctAnswer: APK or Android App Bundle (AAB) depending on delivery
items.explanation: APKs install directly; Play often uses AAB to generate optimized APKs per device.

items.question: What is a Service in Android when long work must run without a foreground UI?
items.correctAnswer: A component for background work, with rules for foreground services when user-visible
items.explanation: Services run app logic in the background; Android restricts background starts for battery and privacy.

items.question: What is an Android Activity primarily responsible for?
items.correctAnswer: A single focused thing the user can do, with a UI window
incorrectAnswers: Only background sync without any UI
The entire Linux kernel on the device
items.explanation: Activities are app components that host screens and participate in the task back stack.

items.question: What is an Intent used for between Android components?
items.correctAnswer: Messaging that requests an action or delivers data
incorrectAnswers: Compiling Kotlin to machine code only
items.explanation: Intents start activities, services, or deliver broadcasts depending on type.

items.question: What is the recommended language for new Android development alongside Java?
items.correctAnswer: Kotlin
items.explanation: Kotlin is the modern default for Android APIs and tooling support.

items.question: In Flutter, what is everything on screen made of?
items.correctAnswer: Widgets
items.explanation: Flutter composes immutable widgets to describe UI; the framework reconciles changes efficiently.

items.question: What does Flutter use to render graphics instead of the platform's native widgets?
items.correctAnswer: Its own rendering engine (Skia/Impeller) drawing pixels
items.explanation: Flutter paints with its engine for consistent cross-platform visuals and performance.

items.question: What does hot reload help developers do during development?
items.correctAnswer: Apply code changes to a running app without losing state when possible
items.explanation: Hot reload injects updates quickly so UI tweaks iterate faster than full restarts.

items.question: What is a `Future` in Dart?
items.correctAnswer: A value or error that completes asynchronously
items.explanation: Futures model async work; Flutter code often awaits I/O and animations.

items.question: What is the `BuildContext` used for in Flutter widget code?
items.correctAnswer: Locating theme, media queries, and ancestor widgets in the tree
items.explanation: Context ties a widget to its position so it can look up inherited widgets and navigation.

items.question: What language do you write Flutter app code in?
items.correctAnswer: Dart
items.explanation: Flutter uses Dart for UI, logic, and plugins with strong tooling support.

items.question: In SwiftUI, what protocol describes a screen or reusable piece of UI?
items.correctAnswer: View
items.explanation: SwiftUI builds interfaces from `View` structs composed into a hierarchy.

items.question: What is Auto Layout used for in iOS interfaces?
items.correctAnswer: Rules that position views across sizes and orientations
items.explanation: Constraints and stacks define flexible layouts that adapt to devices.

items.question: What is Combine in Apple platforms?
items.correctAnswer: A framework for asynchronous events with publishers and subscribers
items.explanation: Combine models streams of values for reactive-style handling alongside Swift concurrency.

items.question: What is a UIViewController mainly responsible for in UIKit?
items.correctAnswer: Managing a view hierarchy and responding to user interaction for one screen
incorrectAnswers: Compiling Swift to LLVM IR only
items.explanation: View controllers coordinate views, layout, and lifecycle events for a screen.

items.question: What is an iOS app sandbox mainly meant to limit?
items.correctAnswer: Access other apps' data and system resources without permission
items.explanation: Sandboxing isolates app storage and capabilities unless entitlements allow more.

items.question: What language did Swift primarily replace for new Apple platform code?
items.correctAnswer: Objective-C
items.explanation: Swift is the modern language for iOS, macOS, and related platforms, interoperating with Objective-C.

items.question: What does Material Design provide for Android and other platforms?
items.correctAnswer: A system of components, motion, and theming guidance
items.explanation: Material defines patterns for buttons, navigation, and adaptive layouts across devices.

items.question: What is progressive disclosure in mobile UX?
items.correctAnswer: Showing advanced options only when needed to reduce overload
incorrectAnswers: Downloading images one pixel per second
items.explanation: Layering complexity keeps first screens simple on small displays.

items.question: What is safe area layout mainly handling on modern phones?
items.correctAnswer: Insets for notches, status bars, and home indicators
items.explanation: Safe areas keep primary content away from system UI and hardware cutouts.

items.question: What problem does a bottom navigation pattern solve?
items.correctAnswer: Thumb-friendly access to a few primary destinations
items.explanation: Placing key actions near the bottom suits one-handed phone use.

items.question: Why do mobile guidelines recommend larger touch targets than precise mouse clicks?
items.correctAnswer: Fingers are less precise and need comfortable hit areas
incorrectAnswers: Because screens have fewer pixels than desktops
items.explanation: Minimum target sizes reduce mis-taps and improve accessibility on small screens.

items.question: Why support dynamic type or font scaling on mobile?
items.correctAnswer: So users with low vision can read comfortably
items.explanation: Respecting user text size settings is a core accessibility practice.

items.question: What bundler does React Native commonly use in development?
items.correctAnswer: Metro
items.explanation: Metro bundles JavaScript for React Native with fast incremental builds.

items.question: What does JSX describe in a React Native component file?
items.correctAnswer: A tree of native-backed components using JavaScript syntax
items.explanation: JSX compiles to `React.createElement` calls that map to native views.

items.question: What is Fast Refresh intended to do during development?
items.correctAnswer: Reload components while preserving state when edits are safe
incorrectAnswers: Delete all app data on every save
items.explanation: Fast Refresh updates the running bundle quickly after source changes.

items.question: What is Hermes in the React Native ecosystem?
items.correctAnswer: A JavaScript engine optimized for mobile startup and memory
items.explanation: Hermes can improve time-to-interactive and bundle characteristics on Android and iOS.

items.question: What is React Native's bridge mainly for?
items.correctAnswer: Letting JavaScript interact with native modules asynchronously
items.explanation: The bridge serializes calls between JS and native code so UI and APIs can integrate safely.

items.question: Why are native modules used in React Native apps?
items.correctAnswer: To access platform APIs and performance-sensitive code not covered by core JS
items.explanation: Native modules expose sensors, camera, and OS features through a defined interface.
